subject: Physics
q:
A tub is filled with water and a wooden cube $10$ cm $\times$ $10$ cm $\times$ $10$ cm is placed in the water. The wooden cube is found to float on the water with a part of it submerged in water. When a metal coin is placed on the wooden cube, the submerged part is increased by $3.87$ cm. The mass of the metal coin is _______ gram.
(Take water density as $1$ g/cm$^3$ and density of wood as $0.4$ g/cm$^3$)
question:
A tub is filled with water and a wooden cube $10$ cm $\times$ $10$ cm $\times$ $10$ cm is placed in the water. The wooden cube is found to float on the water with a part of it submerged in water. When a metal coin is placed on the wooden cube, the submerged part is increased by $3.87$ cm. The mass of the metal coin is _______ gram.
(Take water density as $1$ g/cm$^3$ and density of wood as $0.4$ g/cm$^3$)
solution:
Let $A$ be the base area of the wooden cube.

$A = 10 \text{ cm} \times 10 \text{ cm} = 100 \text{ cm}^2$

Let $\Delta x$ be the increase in the submerged depth of the cube when the coin is placed on it.

$\Delta x = 3.87 \text{ cm}$

The additional volume of water displaced due to the weight of the metal coin is $\Delta V = A \times \Delta x$.

$\Delta V = 100 \times 3.87 = 387 \text{ cm}^3$

By Archimedes' principle, the weight of the metal coin is balanced by the additional buoyant force, which is equal to the weight of the additional water displaced.

$m g = \Delta V \rho_w g$

$m = \Delta V \rho_w$

Substituting the values of $\Delta V$ and the density of water $\rho_w = 1 \text{ g/cm}^3$:

$m = 387 \text{ cm}^3 \times 1 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 387 \text{ g}$

Answer: $387$

subject: Physics
q:
The area of cross-section of a large tank is 0.5 m2. It has a narrow opening near the bottom having area of cross-section 1 cm2. A load of 25 kg is applied on the water at the top in the tank. Neglecting the speed of water in the tank, the velocity of the water, coming out of the opening at the time when the height of water level in the tank is 40 cm above the bottom, will be _____ cm s-1.

[Take g=10 m s-2 ]
question:
The area of cross-section of a large tank is 0.5 m2. It has a narrow opening near the bottom having area of cross-section 1 cm2. A load of 25 kg is applied on the water at the top in the tank. Neglecting the speed of water in the tank, the velocity of the water, coming out of the opening at the time when the height of water level in the tank is 40 cm above the bottom, will be _____ cm s-1.

[Take g=10 m s-2 ]
solution:
Pressure at point 1 is P1=P0, Pressure at point 2 is P2=P0+mgA

Applying Bernoulli's equation at top and bottom

P1+12ρv12+ρgh1=P2+12ρv22+ρgh2⇒P0+12ρv2+0=P0+mgA+0+ρgh

⇒2505+103×10×0.4=12×103×v2

v=3 m s-1=300 cm s-1

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I : If a capillary tube is immersed first in cold water and then in hot water, the height of capillary rise will be smaller in hot water.
Statement II : If a capillary tube is immersed first in cold water and then in hot water, the height of capillary rise will be smaller in cold water.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I : If a capillary tube is immersed first in cold water and then in hot water, the height of capillary rise will be smaller in hot water.
Statement II : If a capillary tube is immersed first in cold water and then in hot water, the height of capillary rise will be smaller in cold water.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate from the options given below
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
For water, the surface tension decreases as the temperature increases. At higher temperatures, the water molecules have more kinetic energy and are better able to overcome the cohesive forces at the surface, resulting in a decrease in surface tension.

So, the height of capillary rise will be smaller in hot water and larger in cold water.

Hence, statement I is true, but statement II is false.

subject: Physics
q: A glass tumbler having inner depth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>17</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is kept on a table. A student starts pouring water <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>μ</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> into it while looking at the surface of water from the above. When he feels that the tumbler is half filled, he stops pouring water. Up to what height, the tumbler is actually filled ?
question: A glass tumbler having inner depth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>17</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is kept on a table. A student starts pouring water <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>μ</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> into it while looking at the surface of water from the above. When he feels that the tumbler is half filled, he stops pouring water. Up to what height, the tumbler is actually filled ?
solution:
Height of water observed by observer
=Hμw=H(4/3)=3H4

Height of air observed by observer =17.5-H

According to question, both height observed by observer is same.
3H4=17.5-H

⇒  H=10 cm

subject: Physics
q: A raindrop with radius R=0.2 mm falls from a cloud at a height h=2000 m above the ground. Assume that the drop is spherical throughout its fall and the force of buoyance may be neglected, then the terminal speed attained by the raindrop is : [Density of water fw=1000 kg m-3 and Density of air fa=1.2 kg m-3, g=10 m/s2 Coefficient of viscosity of air =1.8×10-5N s m-2
question: A raindrop with radius R=0.2 mm falls from a cloud at a height h=2000 m above the ground. Assume that the drop is spherical throughout its fall and the force of buoyance may be neglected, then the terminal speed attained by the raindrop is : [Density of water fw=1000 kg m-3 and Density of air fa=1.2 kg m-3, g=10 m/s2 Coefficient of viscosity of air =1.8×10-5N s m-2
solution:
At terminal speed

a=0

Fnet=0

mg=Fv=6πηRv

V=mg6πηRV

v=ρw4π3R3 g6πηR

=2ρwR2 g9η

=40081 m/s

=4.94 m/s

subject: Physics
q:
An air bubble of volume $2.9 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$ rises from the bottom of a swimming pool of 5 m deep. At the bottom of the pool water temperature is $17^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The volume of the bubble when it reaches the surface, where the water temperature is $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{cm}^{3}$.
$\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right.$, density of water $=10^{3} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$, and 1 atm pressure is $\left.10^{5} \mathrm{~Pa}\right)$
question:
An air bubble of volume $2.9 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$ rises from the bottom of a swimming pool of 5 m deep. At the bottom of the pool water temperature is $17^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The volume of the bubble when it reaches the surface, where the water temperature is $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{cm}^{3}$.
$\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right.$, density of water $=10^{3} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$, and 1 atm pressure is $\left.10^{5} \mathrm{~Pa}\right)$
solution:
An air bubble rises through water with initial volume 2.9 cm³ at the bottom. Calculating pressures at both locations:
At bottom (5 m depth): $P_1 = P_{atm} + \rho gh = 10^5 + 10^3(10)(5) = 1.5 \times 10^5$ Pa
At surface: $P_2 = 10^5$ Pa
Initial temperature: $T_1 = 17°C = 290$ K, Final temperature: $T_2 = 27°C = 300$ K
Using ideal gas law: $\frac{P_1V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2V_2}{T_2}$
$V_2 = V_1 \times \frac{P_1}{P_2} \times \frac{T_2}{T_1} = 2.9 \times \frac{1.5 \times 10^5}{10^5} \times \frac{300}{290}$
$= 2.9 \times 1.5 \times \frac{30}{29} = 2.9 \times 1.5 \times 1.034 \approx 4.5$ cm³

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements :

Statement (I) : Viscosity of gases is greater than that of liquids.

Statement (II) : Surface tension of a liquid decreases due to the presence of insoluble impurities.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :

Statement (I) : Viscosity of gases is greater than that of liquids.

Statement (II) : Surface tension of a liquid decreases due to the presence of insoluble impurities.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: Statement I is correct but statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
solution:
Viscosity is a measure of a fluid's resistance to flow, and gases typically have lower molecular interactions and are less dense than liquids, resulting in lower viscosity.

The presence of insoluble impurities in a liquid can decrease its surface tension. Surface tension is related to the cohesive forces between molecules at the surface of a liquid. Insoluble impurities can disrupt these cohesive forces, leading to a decrease in surface tension.

Hence, this is the correct option.

subject: Physics
q: A pressure-pump has a horizontal tube of cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> for the outflow of water at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The force exerted on the vertical wall just in front of the tube which stops water horizontally flowing out of the tube, is: [given : density of water <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: A pressure-pump has a horizontal tube of cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> for the outflow of water at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The force exerted on the vertical wall just in front of the tube which stops water horizontally flowing out of the tube, is: [given : density of water <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
Volume of water coming out of the pipe per sec =Av=10×10-4×20=0.02 m3 s-1

Mass of water hitting the wall per second m=0.02×103=20 kg s-1

As the water hits the wall normal to the wall and stops, the change of momentum of water hitting the wall is =m0--v=mv

=20×20=400 kg m s-2

Since, the change of momentum obtained is per unit time, it is same as the force.  

Thus, the force acting on the wall by water = force experienced by water jet=400 N.

subject: Physics
q: A spherical soap bubble of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is formed inside another spherical soap bubble of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. If the internal pressure of the smaller bubble of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> in the above system is equal to the internal pressure of the another single soap bubble of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is _____
question: A spherical soap bubble of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is formed inside another spherical soap bubble of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. If the internal pressure of the smaller bubble of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> in the above system is equal to the internal pressure of the another single soap bubble of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is _____
solution:
Pressure inside a bubble is given by, P0+4Tr.

Pressure inside the bubble of smaller radius will be, 

P2-P1=P0+4T6+4T3

Pressure inside the bubble with radius r, P0+4Tr

Now,

⇒P0+4T6+4T3=P0+4Tr

⇒1r=16+13

⇒r=2 cm

subject: Physics
q: A liquid drop of diameter $2$ mm breaks into $512$ droplets. The change in surface energy is $\alpha \times 10^{-6}$ J. The value of $\alpha$ is _______. (Take surface tension of liquid $= 0.08$ N/m)
question: A liquid drop of diameter $2$ mm breaks into $512$ droplets. The change in surface energy is $\alpha \times 10^{-6}$ J. The value of $\alpha$ is _______. (Take surface tension of liquid $= 0.08$ N/m)
solution:
Let the radius of the initial drop be $R$ and the radius of each small droplet be $r$.

Given diameter $D = 2$ mm, so $R = 1$ mm $= 10^{-3}$ m.

By conservation of volume:

$\dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3 = 512 \times \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3$

$R^3 = 512 r^3 \Rightarrow R = 8r \Rightarrow r = \dfrac{R}{8}$

Initial surface area, $A_i = 4\pi R^2$

Final surface area, $A_f = 512 \times 4\pi r^2 = 512 \times 4\pi \left(\dfrac{R}{8}\right)^2 = 32\pi R^2$

Change in surface area, $\Delta A = A_f - A_i = 32\pi R^2 - 4\pi R^2 = 28\pi R^2$

Change in surface energy, $\Delta U = T \Delta A$

$\Delta U = 0.08 \times 28\pi R^2$

Substituting $R = 10^{-3}$ m and $\pi \approx \dfrac{22}{7}$:

$\Delta U = 0.08 \times 28 \times \dfrac{22}{7} \times (10^{-3})^2$

$\Delta U = 0.08 \times 4 \times 22 \times 10^{-6}$

$\Delta U = 7.04 \times 10^{-6}$ J

Comparing with $\alpha \times 10^{-6}$ J, we get $\alpha \approx 7$.

Answer: $7$

subject: Physics
q: A small ball of mass M and density ρ is dropped in a viscous liquid of density ρ0. After some time, the ball falls with a constant velocity. What is the viscous force on the ball?
question: A small ball of mass M and density ρ is dropped in a viscous liquid of density ρ0. After some time, the ball falls with a constant velocity. What is the viscous force on the ball?
solution:
The buoyant force is given by 

B=Vρ0g

where V is the volume of the ball. The weight of the ball

W=Mg=Vρg, is acting in the opposite direction of the buoyant force . Hence, at terminal velocity

Mg-B=Fv

⇒B+Fv=Mg

⇒Fv=Vρg-Vρ0g⇒Fv=ρVg(1-ρ0ρ)⇒Fv=Mg(1-ρ0ρ)

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as

Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : A spherical body of radius (5 ± 0.1) mm having a particular density is falling through a liquid of constant density. The percentage error in the calculation of its terminal velocity is 4%.

Reason R : The terminal velocity of the spherical body falling through the liquid is inversely proportional to its radius. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as

Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : A spherical body of radius (5 ± 0.1) mm having a particular density is falling through a liquid of constant density. The percentage error in the calculation of its terminal velocity is 4%.

Reason R : The terminal velocity of the spherical body falling through the liquid is inversely proportional to its radius. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
solution:
The terminal velocity is 

VT=29r2gρ-ρ'η⇒VT∝r2

Differentiating and dividing by VT,

⇒dVTVT=2drr=2×0.15

So, the percentage error is

dVTVT×100=0.25×100=4%

As can be seen the terminal velocity is directly proportional to the square of the radius. Hence A is true but R is false.

q: The velocity of a small ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> and density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>d</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>, when dropped in a container filled with glycerine, becomes constant after some time. If the density of glycerine is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>d</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, then the viscous force acting on the ball, will be
question: The velocity of a small ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> and density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>d</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>, when dropped in a container filled with glycerine, becomes constant after some time. If the density of glycerine is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>d</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, then the viscous force acting on the ball, will be
solution:
At the time of terminal velocity ball is in equilibrium i.e. Fg=Fb+Fv

⇒Fv=Fg-Fb

⇒Fv=mg-d2md1g

⇒mg1-d2d1

subject: Physics
q: In Millikan's oil drop experiment, what is viscous force acting on an uncharged drop of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>? Take viscosity of liquid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> (Neglect buoyancy due to air).
question: In Millikan's oil drop experiment, what is viscous force acting on an uncharged drop of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>? Take viscosity of liquid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> (Neglect buoyancy due to air).
solution:
Use the concept the viscous force is the same as the weight of the body, 

⇒F=mg=ρ×43πr3×g=3.9×10-10 N.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: Statement I : When speed of liquid is zero everywhere, pressure difference at any two points depends on equation $P_1-P_2=\rho g\left(h_2-h_1\right)$. Statement II : In ventury tube shown $2 \mathrm{gh}=v_1^2-v_2^2$
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements: Statement I : When speed of liquid is zero everywhere, pressure difference at any two points depends on equation $P_1-P_2=\rho g\left(h_2-h_1\right)$. Statement II : In ventury tube shown $2 \mathrm{gh}=v_1^2-v_2^2$
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct.
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect.
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct.
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect.
solution:
Applying Bernoulli's equation $\mathrm{P}_1+\rho \mathrm{gh}_1+\frac{1}{2} \rho \mathrm{v}_1^2=\mathrm{P}_2+\rho \mathrm{gh}_2+\frac{1}{2} \rho \mathrm{v}_2^2$ $\left[h_1 \& h_2\right.$ are height of point from any reference level] Given $\mathrm{V}_1=\mathrm{V}_2=0$ (for statement-1) $\therefore \mathrm{P}_1-\mathrm{P}_2=\mathrm{g}\left(\mathrm{h}_2-\mathrm{h}_2\right)$
For statement-2 $\mathrm{P}_1+\frac{1}{2} \rho \mathrm{v}_1^2=\mathrm{P}_2+\frac{1}{2} \rho \mathrm{v}_2^2$ $\begin{aligned} & P_1-P_2=\rho g h \\ & P_1-P_2=\frac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2-\frac{1}{2} \rho v_1^2 \\ & \rho g h=\frac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2-\frac{1}{2} \rho v_1^2 \\ & 2 g h=v_2^2-v_1^2 \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The surface of water in a water tank of cross section area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>750</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> on the top of a house is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. above the tap level. The speed of water coming out through the tap of cross section area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. At that instant, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be ______.
question: The surface of water in a water tank of cross section area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>750</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> on the top of a house is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. above the tap level. The speed of water coming out through the tap of cross section area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. At that instant, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be ______.
solution:
Using continuity equation

A1v1=A2v2

We have, 

750×10-4v1=500×10-6×0.3

v1=500×3×10-3750 

=2×10-3 m s-1

Thus, dhdt=-2×10-3 m s-1

Hence, the value of x=2.

subject: Physics
q:
Pressure inside a soap bubble is greater than the pressure outside by an amount :
(given : $\mathrm{R}=$ Radius of bubble $\mathrm{S}=$ Surface tension of bubble)
question:
Pressure inside a soap bubble is greater than the pressure outside by an amount :
(given : $\mathrm{R}=$ Radius of bubble $\mathrm{S}=$ Surface tension of bubble)
solution:
There are two liquid-air surfaces in bubble so
$\Delta \mathrm{P}=2\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{~S}}{\mathrm{R}}\right)=\frac{4 \mathrm{~S}}{\mathrm{R}}$

subject: Physics
q: Two liquids A and B have $\theta_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\theta_{\mathrm{B}}$ as contact angles in a capillary tube. If $K=\cos \theta_A / \cos \theta_B$. then identify the correct statement:
question: Two liquids A and B have $\theta_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\theta_{\mathrm{B}}$ as contact angles in a capillary tube. If $K=\cos \theta_A / \cos \theta_B$. then identify the correct statement:
options: $K$ is negative, then liquid $A$ and liquid $B$ have convex meniscus.
$K$ is negative, then liquid $A$ and liquid $B$ have concave meniscus.
K is negative, then liquid A has concave meniscus and liquid $B$ has convex meniscus
K is zero, then liquid A has convex meniscus and liquid B has concave meniscus.
solution:
$\mathrm{k}=\frac{\cos \theta_{\mathrm{A}}}{\cos \theta_{\mathrm{B}}}$
It is negative when $\cos \theta_A \& \cos \theta_B$ are of opposite sign. so option (3)

subject: Physics
q: A spherical body of radius $r$ and density $\sigma$ falls freely through a viscous liquid having density $\rho$ and viscosity $\eta$ and attains a terminal velocity $v_{0}$. Estimated maximum error in the quantity $\eta$ is : (Ignore errors associated with $\sigma, \rho$ and $g$, gravitational acceleration)
question: A spherical body of radius $r$ and density $\sigma$ falls freely through a viscous liquid having density $\rho$ and viscosity $\eta$ and attains a terminal velocity $v_{0}$. Estimated maximum error in the quantity $\eta$ is : (Ignore errors associated with $\sigma, \rho$ and $g$, gravitational acceleration)
solution:
The terminal velocity $v_0$ of a spherical body falling through a viscous liquid is given by Stokes' Law:
$v_0 = \frac{2r^2(\sigma - \rho)g}{9\eta}$
Rearranging the formula to express viscosity $\eta$ in terms of other variables:
$\eta = \frac{2r^2(\sigma - \rho)g}{9v_0}$
Since we are told to ignore errors associated with $\sigma$, $\rho$, and $g$, these quantities along with the numerical factor $\frac{2}{9}$ are treated as constants.
The expression for $\eta$ can be written as $\eta = k \cdot \frac{r^2}{v_0}$, where $k = \frac{2(\sigma - \rho)g}{9}$ is a constant.
Taking the natural logarithm on both sides:
$\ln \eta = \ln k + 2\ln r - \ln v_0$
Differentiating both sides to find the relative error:
$\frac{d\eta}{\eta} = 0 + 2\frac{dr}{r} - \frac{dv_0}{v_0}$
For the estimated maximum fractional error, we take the absolute sum of the individual relative errors:
$\frac{\Delta \eta}{\eta} = 2\frac{\Delta r}{r} + \frac{\Delta v_0}{v_0}$
This matches option (1).

subject: Physics
q: A thin transparent film with refractive index 1.4 , is held on circular ring of radius 1.8 cm . The fluid in the film evaporates such that transmission through the film at wavelength 560 nm goes to a minimum every 12 seconds. Assuming that the film is flat on its two sides, the rate of evaporation is ____ $\pi \times 10^{-13} \mathrm{~m}^3 / \mathrm{s}$.
question: A thin transparent film with refractive index 1.4 , is held on circular ring of radius 1.8 cm . The fluid in the film evaporates such that transmission through the film at wavelength 560 nm goes to a minimum every 12 seconds. Assuming that the film is flat on its two sides, the rate of evaporation is ____ $\pi \times 10^{-13} \mathrm{~m}^3 / \mathrm{s}$.
solution:
For a thin film interference, a fringe for transmission is formed.
When
$2 \mu x=n \lambda$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & \frac{d x}{d t}=\left(\frac{d n}{d t}\right) \frac{\lambda}{2 \mu} \\ & =\left(\frac{1}{12}\right) \frac{560 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 1.4}=\frac{5}{3} \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & V=\text { Volume of film }=\pi R^2 x \\ & \frac{d V}{d t}=\pi R^2 \frac{d x}{d t} \\ & =\pi\left(1.8 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2 \frac{5}{3} \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~m}^3 / \mathrm{s} \\ & =54 \pi \times 10^{-13} \mathrm{~m}^3 / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A sphere of relative density $\sigma$ and diameter $D$ has concentric cavity of diameter $d$. The ratio of $\frac{D}{d}$, if it just floats on water in a tank is :
question: A sphere of relative density $\sigma$ and diameter $D$ has concentric cavity of diameter $d$. The ratio of $\frac{D}{d}$, if it just floats on water in a tank is :
solution:
weight $(w)=\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(\frac{\mathrm{D}^3-\mathrm{d}^3}{8}\right) \sigma \mathrm{g}$
Buoyant force $\left(\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{b}}\right)=1 \times \frac{4}{3} \pi\left(\frac{\mathrm{D}^3}{8}\right) \cdot \mathrm{g}$
For Just Float $\Rightarrow \mathrm{w}=\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{b}}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow\left(D^3-d^3\right) \sigma=D^3 \\ & \Rightarrow 1-\frac{d^3}{D^3}=\frac{1}{\sigma} \\ & \Rightarrow 1-\frac{1}{\sigma}=\left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^3 \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\sigma}{\sigma-1}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}=\left(\frac{D}{d}\right)\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> is the density and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>η</mi></math> is coefficient of viscosity of fluid which flows with a speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> in the pipe of diameter <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math>, the correct formula for Reynolds number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> is
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> is the density and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>η</mi></math> is coefficient of viscosity of fluid which flows with a speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> in the pipe of diameter <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math>, the correct formula for Reynolds number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> is
solution:
Reynolds number is given by, Re=ρvdη, where, v=flow velocity, d=hydraulic diameter of the pipe, ρ=density of the fluid & η=coefficient of viscosity of the fluid.

Reynolds number is the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces within a fluid which is subjected to relative internal movement due to different fluid velocities.

subject: Physics
q: The surface tension of soap solution is  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The amount of work done required to increase the radius of soap bubble from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is _____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> . (take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>22</mn><mn>7</mn></mfrac></math>)
question: The surface tension of soap solution is  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The amount of work done required to increase the radius of soap bubble from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is _____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> . (take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>22</mn><mn>7</mn></mfrac></math>)
solution:
The amount of work done is equal to the change in the surface energy to increase the radius of the bubble.

The formula to calculate the change in surface energy is given by

 ΔU=2(T×ΔA)   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) to calculate the required work done.

∆U=3.5×10-2×4π(0.2)2-(0.1)2×2 J=3.5×10-2×4×227×0.03×2=2×1.32×10-2=264×10-4 J

subject: Physics
q: A big drop is formed by coalescing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn></math> small droplets of water. The surface energy will become :
question: A big drop is formed by coalescing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn></math> small droplets of water. The surface energy will become :
solution:
Lets say radius of small droplets is r and that of big drop is R. Total volume of the drops will remains unchanged. Therefore, 

43πR3=100043πr3

⇒R=10r

Now, initial surface energy is, Ui=10004πr2 S and the final surface energy is, 

Uf=4πR2 S

=1004πr2S

Clearly, Uf=110Ui.

subject: Physics
q: A liquid of density $600$ kg/m$^3$ flowing steadily in a tube of varying cross-section. The cross-section at a point $A$ is $1.0$ cm$^2$ and that at $B$ is $20$ mm$^2$. Both the points $A$ and $B$ are in same horizontal plane, the speed of the liquid at $A$ is $10$ cm/s. The difference in pressures at $A$ and $B$ points is ________ Pa.
question: A liquid of density $600$ kg/m$^3$ flowing steadily in a tube of varying cross-section. The cross-section at a point $A$ is $1.0$ cm$^2$ and that at $B$ is $20$ mm$^2$. Both the points $A$ and $B$ are in same horizontal plane, the speed of the liquid at $A$ is $10$ cm/s. The difference in pressures at $A$ and $B$ points is ________ Pa.
solution:
Using the equation of continuity:

$A_A v_A = A_B v_B$

$(1.0 \times 10^{-4}) \times 0.1 = (20 \times 10^{-6}) \times v_B$

$v_B = \dfrac{10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.5$ m/s

Using Bernoulli's equation for a horizontal tube:

$P_A + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v_A^2 = P_B + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v_B^2$

$P_A - P_B = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho (v_B^2 - v_A^2)$

Substituting the given values:

$P_A - P_B = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 600 \times (0.5^2 - 0.1^2)$

$P_A - P_B = 300 \times (0.25 - 0.01)$

$P_A - P_B = 300 \times 0.24 = 72$ Pa

Answer: $72$

subject: Physics
q: A ball is made of a material of density $\rho$ where $\rho_{\text {oil }} < \rho < \rho_{\text {water }}$ with $\rho_{\text {oil }}$ and $\rho_{\text {water }}$ representing the densities of oil and water, respectively. The oil and water are immiscible. If the above ball is in equilibrium in a mixture of this oil and water, which of the following pictures represents its equilibrium position?
question: A ball is made of a material of density $\rho$ where $\rho_{\text {oil }} < \rho < \rho_{\text {water }}$ with $\rho_{\text {oil }}$ and $\rho_{\text {water }}$ representing the densities of oil and water, respectively. The oil and water are immiscible. If the above ball is in equilibrium in a mixture of this oil and water, which of the following pictures represents its equilibrium position?
solution:
$$
\rho_{\text {oil }} < \rho < \rho_{\text {water }}
$$
Oil is the least dense of them so it should settle at the top with water at the base. Now the ball is denser than oil but less denser than water. So, it will sink through oil but will not sink in water. So it will stay at the oil-water interface.

subject: Physics
q: A tube of length 1 m is filled completely with an ideal liquid of mass 2 M , and closed at both ends. The tube is rotated uniformly in horizontal plane about one of its ends. If the force exerted by the liquid at the other end is F then angular velocity of the tube is $\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\alpha \mathrm{M}}}$ in SI unit. The value of $\alpha$ is __________.
question: A tube of length 1 m is filled completely with an ideal liquid of mass 2 M , and closed at both ends. The tube is rotated uniformly in horizontal plane about one of its ends. If the force exerted by the liquid at the other end is F then angular velocity of the tube is $\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\alpha \mathrm{M}}}$ in SI unit. The value of $\alpha$ is __________.
solution: \(\begin{aligned} & F=m \omega^2 r_{\mathrm{cm}} \\ & r_{\mathrm{cm}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m} \\ & \omega=\sqrt{\frac{2 F}{m}} \\ & m=2 M \\ & \omega=\sqrt{\frac{F}{M}} \\ & \alpha=1\end{aligned}\)

The inner radius of the shell is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo> </mo><mo>.</mo></math> If the specific gravity of the shell material is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>27</mn><mn>8</mn></mfrac></math> with respect to water, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is:
question: A hollow spherical shell at outer radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> floats just submerged under the water surface. The inner radius of the shell is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo> </mo><mo>.</mo></math> If the specific gravity of the shell material is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>27</mn><mn>8</mn></mfrac></math> with respect to water, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is:
solution:
In equilibrium mg=FB

43πR3-r3ρ0g=43πR3ρwg

1-rR3278ρw=ρw

1-r3R3=927

1-13=r3R3

23=r3R3

rR=231/3

1-r3R3=827

r3R3=1-827=1927

r=0.89

q: A tube of length $L$ is shown in the figure. The radius of cross section at the point (1) is 2 cm and at the point (2) is 1 cm , respectively. If the velocity of water entering at point (1) is $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, then velocity of water leaving the point (2) will be
question: A tube of length $L$ is shown in the figure. The radius of cross section at the point (1) is 2 cm and at the point (2) is 1 cm , respectively. If the velocity of water entering at point (1) is $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, then velocity of water leaving the point (2) will be
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{A}_1 \mathrm{~V}_1=\mathrm{A}_2 \mathrm{~V}_2 \Rightarrow 2 \pi(2 \mathrm{R})^2=\mathrm{V}_2 \pi \mathrm{R}^2 \\ & \therefore \mathrm{~V}_2=8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A cube of ice floats partly in water and partly in kerosene oil. The ratio of volume of ice immersed in water to that in kerosene oil (specific gravity of Kerosene oil $=0.8$, specific gravity of ice $=0.9$)
question: A cube of ice floats partly in water and partly in kerosene oil. The ratio of volume of ice immersed in water to that in kerosene oil (specific gravity of Kerosene oil $=0.8$, specific gravity of ice $=0.9$)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{v}_1=\text { volume immersed in water. } \\ & \mathrm{v}_2=\text { volume immersed in oil. } \\ & \mathrm{v}_1 \rho_{\mathrm{w}} \mathrm{g}+\mathrm{v}_2 \rho_{\mathrm{o}} \mathrm{g}=\left(\mathrm{v}_1+\mathrm{v}_2\right) \rho_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{g} \\ & \mathrm{v}_1+\frac{\mathrm{v}_2 \rho_{\mathrm{o}}}{\rho_{\mathrm{w}}}=\left(\mathrm{v}_1+\mathrm{v}_2\right) \frac{\rho_{\mathrm{c}}}{\rho_{\mathrm{w}}} \\ & =\mathrm{v}_1+0.8 \mathrm{v}_2=0.9 \mathrm{v}_1+0.9 \mathrm{v}_2 \\ & =0.1 \mathrm{v}_1=0.1 \mathrm{v}_2 \\ & \mathrm{v}_1: \mathrm{v}_2=1: 1\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Small water droplets of radius $0.01 \mathrm{~mm}$ are formed in the upper atmosphere and falling with a terminal velocity of $10 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$. Due to condensation, if 8 such droplets are coalesced and formed a larger drop, the new terminal velocity will be _____$\mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{s}$.
question: Small water droplets of radius $0.01 \mathrm{~mm}$ are formed in the upper atmosphere and falling with a terminal velocity of $10 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$. Due to condensation, if 8 such droplets are coalesced and formed a larger drop, the new terminal velocity will be _____$\mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{s}$.
solution:
$\mathrm{m}=$ mass of small drop
$\mathrm{M}=$ mass of bigger drop
$\begin{aligned} & V_t=\frac{2}{9} \frac{R^2(\rho-\sigma) g}{\eta} \\ & 8 \propto m=M \\ & 8 r^3=R^3 \Rightarrow R=2 R\end{aligned}$
as $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{t}} \times \mathrm{R}^2 \quad \because$ Radius double so $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{t}}$ becomes 4 time
$\therefore 4 \times 10=40 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$

subject: Physics
q: A thin uniform tube is bent into a circle of radius $r$ in the virtical plane. Equal volumes of two immiscible liquids, whose densities are $\rho_1$ and $\rho_2\left(\rho_1>\rho_2\right)$ fill half the circle. The angle $\theta$ between the radius vector passing through the common interface and the vertical is
question: A thin uniform tube is bent into a circle of radius $r$ in the virtical plane. Equal volumes of two immiscible liquids, whose densities are $\rho_1$ and $\rho_2\left(\rho_1>\rho_2\right)$ fill half the circle. The angle $\theta$ between the radius vector passing through the common interface and the vertical is
solution:
Pressure at interface A must be same from both the sides to be in equilibrium.
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad &(\mathrm{R} \cos \theta+\mathrm{R} \sin \theta) \rho_2 \mathrm{~g} \\ &=(\mathrm{R} \cos \theta-\mathrm{R} \sin \theta) \rho_1 \mathrm{~g} \end{aligned}$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\rho_1}{\rho_2}=\frac{\cos \theta+\sin \theta}{\cos \theta-\sin \theta}=\frac{1+\tan \theta}{1-\tan \theta}$ $\Rightarrow \rho_1-\rho_1 \tan \theta=\rho_2+\rho_2 \tan \theta$ $\Rightarrow \quad\left(\rho_1+\rho_2\right) \tan \theta=\rho_1-\rho_2$ $\therefore \quad \theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\rho_1-\rho_2}{\rho_1+\rho_2}\right)$, soclosest answer is (a)

subject: Physics
q:
A water drop of radius 1 cm is broken into 729 equal droplets. If surface tension of water is 75dyne cm-1, then the gain in surface energy upto first decimal place will be

[Given π=3.14]
question:
A water drop of radius 1 cm is broken into 729 equal droplets. If surface tension of water is 75dyne cm-1, then the gain in surface energy upto first decimal place will be

[Given π=3.14]
solution:
The volume of the bigger drop will be, V=43πR3. If the radius of smaller drops is r, using the conservation of volume,

n43πr3=43πR3⇒729×43πr3=43πR3⇒r=R9

The initial surface energy will be,

Ei=4πR2T 

And final surface energy will be,

Ef=n4πr2T=729×4πR92T=36πR2T

Therefore, the change in the surface energy will be,

⇒ΔE=Ef-Ei=32πR2T=32×3.14×1×10-22×75×10-3⇒ΔE=7.5×10-4 J
